nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; tends to occupy holes and crevices of seaward reefs from the surge zone to depths of around 26 m

diet.notes: Generally feeds on meaty foods such as silversides, krill, shrimp, and crustacean flesh; typically offered 2–3 substantial meals per week, delivered on a feeding stick due to poor eyesight and powerful jaws.

summary: The Dwarf Moray Eel (Gymnothorax melatremus) is a small, secretive reef eel from the Indo-Pacific, generally reaching around 30 cm. Often sold as the Golden Dwarf Moray Eel, it tends to hide among live rock during the day and is considered semi-aggressive toward small fish and crustaceans.
careNotes: The Dwarf Moray Eel is also widely sold under the name Golden Dwarf Moray Eel, which refers to the species' characteristic whitish-yellow to bright golden body coloration and is not a distinct morph. It is further recognized by variable brown spotting and distinctive faint pale-blue 'cat eyes'. In the aquarium, the species tends to be primarily nocturnal, spending most daylight hours tucked into caves, crevices, or PVC tubes, and becoming more active at night. This species is widely considered an escape specialist — a tightly-fitting, gap-free aquarium cover is generally regarded as essential. Feeding is best done with a feeding stick rather than by hand, given its poor eyesight and sharp teeth. Strong biological filtration and a protein skimmer are generally advised, as its carnivorous diet produces significant waste. The species is reported to be solitary and secretive in the wild, and conspecific aggression (aggression toward others of the same species) is commonly observed.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (30 cm confirmed), native range, taxonomy (Muraenidae, Anguilliformes), and temperature range. FishBase reports environmental temperature of 25.4–28.9°C vs LiveAquaria's 22.2–25.6°C; filed range is the average.
